pi_items.source: Verified in setup_task.sh line 6 and export_result.sh line 5: Windows path for data directory
Verified in setup_task.sh lines 6, 64: CSV file is generated and moved to target Windows directory

CDC Epi Info 7 Visual Dashboard documentation confirms canvas files use .cvs7 extension, not .canvas7. See CDC official user guide at https://www.cdc.gov/epiinfo/user-guide/visual-dashboard/opensavesend.html which references Oswego.cvs7 and Ecoli.cvs7 format
Verified in export_result.sh line 6 and verifier.py line 65: text file to contain outlier findings
Code injection verified in setup_task.sh line 50: 'if pid == 450:' - PatientID 450 receives injected outlier values
Code injection verified in setup_task.sh line 51: 'bmi = 175.6' - impossible BMI value for an adult human (normal range ~18-30)
Code injection verified in setup_task.sh line 52: 'gluc = 125' - normal glucose value for contrast with extreme BMI outlier
Verified in verifier.py line 34: matches the injected outlier_id value
Verified in verifier.py line 35: matches the injected outlier_bmi value

privileged_info_summary: The task uses synthetic data: 500 patient records generated via Python random distributions with a deliberate outlier injected at PatientID 450. The outlier has an impossible BMI value of 175.6 (realistic BMI range is 15-60) with normal glucose of 125. Epi Info 7 is a real CDC epidemiology software tool. However, one critical error exists: the metadata specifies dashboard filename as 'qa_dashboard.canvas7', but CDC Epi Info 7 documentation confirms the correct extension is '.cvs7' (as seen in examples like 'Oswego.cvs7' and 'Ecoli.cvs7'). The data contains 10 columns (PatientID, Pregnancies, Glucose, BloodPressure, SkinThickness, Insulin, BMI, DiabetesPedigreeFunction, Age, Outcome).
